FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study), US-based, 9-10 year old baseline sample (8267 included in analysis)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen modalities: texting, video chat, social media (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter), television, video games, videos (YouTube) — all separately measured per B1; fulfills YSTS criterion (B1)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between screen time (independent) and MIND diet score (dependent) at year 1; both prospective and cross-sectional regressions with separate β coefficients reported for each modality (Table 2)

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients reported in main tables (Table 2, Table 3): e.g., total screen time β = -0.07 (95% CI -0.09, -0.05) prospective, -0.07 cross-sectional; television β = -0.14 to -0.07; social media β = -0.16 cross-sectional; texting β = -0.15 cross-sectional

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data; prospective and cross-sectional analysis with regression coefficients linking screen modalities to nutrition outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication of duplicate (preprint, abstract, or deposit). Published in Academic Pediatrics 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply — SM/smartphone are independent variables predicting nutrition outcome (MIND diet score), not dependent variables. Analysis does not fit EC7 definition (B18: SM-as-DV analyses disqualify for EC7 when SM-as-IV exists)
notes: B7 applied: TV included but combined with qualifying modalities (texting, videos, social media, video games) as separate coefficients. Screen modalities are device-based and include smartphone use types (texting, social media), thus qualifying. MIND diet is comprehensive nutrition outcome separate from screen use.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up data; N = 9859 early adolescents aged 12-13 years, US-based, nationally representative sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen modalities separately measured: television/movies, video games (single and multi-player), texting, social media, internet — all smartphone/device-based per B1; qualifies under YSTS separate-coefficient rule (B1)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and screen time modalities and problematic screen use (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, Social Media Addiction Questionnaire, Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). β coefficients reported for each modality (Table 2, Table 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients (95% CI) reported in main tables: e.g., transgender vs cisgender total screen time B = 4.51 (95% CI 1.17-7.85); television B = 0.82 (0.16, 1.49); video games single-player B = 0.89 (0.36, 1.42); social media B = 0.83 (0.06, 1.61); texting B = 0.81 (0.04, 1.59)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study Year 3 data; cross-sectional regression analyses linking gender identity to screen use outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate indicated. Published in Annals of Epidemiology 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply — SM/screen use are dependent variables (predicted by gender identity), not independent variables predicting only demographic outcomes. Analysis focuses on demographic (gender identity) → screen use associations, opposite direction of EC7 definition
notes: Cross-sectional analysis. Gender identity/transgender status is the independent variable; screen time and problematic use are dependent variables. Separate β coefficients reported for each modality per B1 (YSTS criterion). Propensity weights applied based on American Community Survey.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Years 2-3 follow-up; N = 9,398 early adolescents aged 11-12 years at baseline, US-based nationally representative sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen modalities separately measured at bedtime: watching movies/videos/TV, video games, music, talking/texting on phone, social media, chat rooms, internet browsing, computer/laptop use, reading; texting and social media are qualifying SM/phone variables per B1; all separately assessed (nine-item measure)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression analyses (logistic, ordinal logistic, linear) examining prospective associations between bedtime screen use (Year 2) and sleep outcomes (Year 3). Separate coefficients (OR, B) reported for each bedtime activity and sleep measure (Table 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients, OR (95% CI), and p-values reported in main table (Table 3) for all analyses: e.g., TV/device in bedroom B = -0.21 (95% CI -0.28, -0.13) for sleep duration; leaving ringer on OR = 1.25 (95% CI 1.00, 1.56) for sleep disturbance; texting B = -0.17 (95% CI -0.22, -0.12); social media B = -0.21 (95% CI -0.26, -0.16)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis of ABCD Study data; regression coefficients examining bedtime screen use predictors of sleep outcomes one year later

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate indicated. Published in Journal of Adolescent Health 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply — SM/screen use are independent variables predicting sleep outcomes (non-SM outcome), opposite of EC7 definition (which requires SM as dependent variable only). Prospective analysis with demographic covariates but SM/bedtime screen use are predictors, not outcomes
notes: Prospective cohort design with one-year follow-up. Multiple bedtime screen activities separately assessed and reported with distinct coefficients (B1 YSTS criterion met). Texting and social media included as qualifying SM/phone variables. Sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration) are non-SM outcomes. Adjusted for confounders including depression, ACEs, melatonin use.
